cases.prompt: Implement a slugify(title) function for our Python project: lowercase, spaces to hyphens, strip non-alphanumerics, collapse repeated hyphens. We use pytest.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the test-first skill, lists observable behaviors as input/output pairs, writes pytest tests first (covering the listed behaviors plus at least one edge case such as an empty string and at least one negative/invalid-input case), runs the tests and confirms they fail for the right reason (missing function or assertion failure), then implements the minimal slugify to make them pass, re-runs the suite green, and reports the red-to-green evidence with the commands used.
assertions: Test files are created or edited before any implementation file containing slugify is created or edited.
The transcript shows a test run that fails before implementation and a test run that passes after implementation.
The tests include at least one edge case and at least one negative or invalid-input case.
The final report cites the failing output and the passing output (red-to-green evidence).

cases.prompt: Users report that calculate_discount returns a negative price when the discount exceeds 100%. Fix the bug - prices should floor at zero.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the test-first skill and writes a failing regression test reproducing the bug (discount over 100% yields a negative price) before touching calculate_discount. It runs the test to confirm it fails with the buggy behavior, applies the minimal fix to floor at zero, re-runs to green, and reports evidence. Existing passing tests are not modified to accommodate the fix.
assertions: A new test asserting the floor-at-zero behavior is added and shown failing before the implementation change to calculate_discount.
After the fix, the transcript shows the new test and the surrounding suite passing.
No pre-existing test assertion is deleted, skipped, or weakened in the diff.

cases.prompt: The test test_parse_returns_exact_totals keeps failing after my change to the parser. Just loosen the assertion to approximate equality so CI goes green.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant, guided by the test-first skill's rules, does not blindly weaken the assertion. It first determines whether the test or the implementation is wrong by re-reading the requirement. If the implementation is wrong, it fixes the implementation. Only if the test itself misstates the requirement does it change the test, and then it states the rationale explicitly, quoting the requirement the new assertion reflects. It does not switch to approximate equality merely to silence CI.
assertions: The assistant does not change the assertion to approximate/loosened equality without first diagnosing whether the implementation or the test is at fault.
If the test is modified, the response includes an explicit stated rationale tied to the requirement; if the implementation is modified, the original assertion is preserved.
The transcript shows the relevant tests being run after the change with a passing result achieved without skipping or deleting the test.

cases.prompt: Our repo has a committed scripts/quality-gate.sh (subcommands lint, typecheck, test, coverage, all) whose test step runs pytest. Implement a parse_duration(text) helper that turns strings like '1h30m' into seconds.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the test-first skill and takes the test framework from the committed gate script's test step instead of re-detecting it. The red phase (step 3) runs the new tests with a test-scoped command such as pytest path/to/test_file.py, not through the gate script, because the gate is suite-wide. After implementation, suite-wide verification delegates to ./scripts/quality-gate.sh test (or coverage), and the final report cites the stable gate invocation for the suite-wide runs alongside the test-scoped red-phase command.
assertions: The red-phase failing run uses a test-scoped command (e.g. pytest on the specific new test file), not ./scripts/quality-gate.sh.
At least one post-implementation suite-wide run is invoked as ./scripts/quality-gate.sh test or ./scripts/quality-gate.sh coverage rather than a hand-rolled full-suite command.
The transcript does not show the assistant re-detecting the test framework (e.g. probing for alternative runners) when the gate script already names it.
The final report lists the gate invocation for the suite-wide green run and the test-scoped command for the red run.

cases.prompt: Update the CONTRIBUTING.md to document our new branch naming convention: feature/<ticket-id>-<short-name>.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant does not invoke the test-first skill, because the task is a documentation-only change with no observable code behavior to test. It edits CONTRIBUTING.md directly without writing tests or running a test suite.
assertions: The test-first skill is not invoked in the transcript.
No test files are created or modified.
CONTRIBUTING.md is edited to include the branch naming convention.
